completeness.rationale: All seven expected extracts are present: 2-4 hour delay, ~1 week onset, morning-worse/afternoon-better pattern, Daily Digest feature as the suspected batch process, queue/pipeline contention, transactional vs. bulk competition, and the original 1-2 minute baseline. The summary additionally captures severity, impact, reproduction steps, and a proposed test case.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches acceptable diagnostic path #2: the agent identified the Daily Digest feature as the cause and explicitly described queue contention ('shared email-sending job whose throughput is saturated by digest processing'). The recommended fix even arrives at the core resolution — prioritizing immediate notifications over digest compilation — which maps directly to the actual priority-inversion fix. No material inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked two well-chosen rounds of questions and resolved only after the reporter volunteered the critical Daily Digest clue. One obvious follow-up was skipped: asking specifically when the Daily Digest sends (which would have confirmed the 9am correlation). The agent acknowledged remaining gaps in the information_gaps field, showing awareness. Given the reporter's non-admin status, further backend questions would have been unproductive.

economy.rationale: Both question sets were productive. The email-provider question in round one was slightly off-target (Exchange was quickly ruled out), but it served to rule out a plausible alternative and was not wasted. No redundant or repeated questions across turns.

question_quality.rationale: Round one effectively anchored the version and change-timeline, surfacing the 'product update about a week ago' correlation. Round two showed genuine diagnostic reasoning by asking for send-vs-created timestamps to confirm server-side queuing — a non-obvious but highly targeted ask. Questions were professional and led the reporter to volunteer the Daily Digest observation unprompted, which was the pivotal clue.

actionability.rationale: The summary is immediately actionable: it names the feature (Daily Digest), describes the mechanism (saturated shared email-sending job at 9am), gives three concrete investigation steps, specifies what to look for in delivery logs (notification_created_at vs. notification_sent_at), and provides a precise post-fix test case with exact times. A developer could open the queue code and start investigating within minutes.

notable_strengths: Connected the morning-peak delay pattern to a likely batch/digest job without needing backend access, arriving at the correct acceptable diagnostic path
Recommended fix explicitly mentions prioritizing immediate notifications over digest — essentially the actual fix — derived purely from user-facing clues
Triage summary includes a concrete proposed test case with specific times, making verification straightforward after a fix is deployed
Correctly and quickly ruled out Exchange as the bottleneck, narrowing scope to TaskFlow's sending pipeline
notable_weaknesses: Did not follow up on the Daily Digest timing (when does it send? how many recipients?) — one additional question could have locked in the 9am causation more explicitly
Did not ask whether other team members experience identical delays, which would have confirmed the issue is systemic rather than per-user
most_insightful_question: In TaskFlow's admin panel, is there a notification queue or delivery log that shows when notifications were *sent* versus when they were *created*? — This question targeted the exact artifact (send vs. create timestamp gap) that would definitively confirm server-side queuing delay, demonstrating real diagnostic depth.
missed_opportunities: Ask specifically what time the new Daily Digest email arrives in the inbox — this would have directly linked the 9am batch send to the delay window
Ask whether other team members or colleagues experience the same delays, to confirm the issue is account-agnostic and systemic
